column1: # THE LONG NIGHT — juice
# Owner: Jane. Machine-readable. John: parse this, don't hardcode the feel.
# Tab-separated. '#' comments and blank lines ignored. Row kinds: param, shake, glyph.
# See design.md §14. This table exists because "juice" is the last system where a
# programmer is expected to pick numbers by taste, and the numbers ARE the taste.
# "Singular characters walking around... is this the 1960s? ASCII art can be made
# much more impressive nowadays." He is not asking for more glyphs. He is asking
# for the glyphs to be ALIVE. A `g` that flinches when you hit it and pops when it
# dies is more impressive than a `g` drawn in three colours, and it costs no art.
# ---- EVERY NUMBER HERE IS IN SECONDS, NEVER IN FRAMES ------------------------
# He also asked for 120fps. A flash written as "2 frames" is twice as fast at 120
# as at 60, and that is why old ports feel wrong on new hardware. Nothing in this
# table is a frame count. If you find yourself writing `--framesLeft`, stop.
# Juice is the FOURTH thing on the screen. The first three -- you, the XP, the
# thing about to touch you -- already have a law (§9, the luminance ladder), and
# juice does not get to break it just because it is pretty:
#   Juice may never take a cell that would otherwise show the player, an XP mote,
#   or an enemy; and it may never be brighter than the thing it is celebrating.
# The one licensed exception is `hit_flash_lift`, below, and it is bounded to 60ms.
# 1. HIT FLASH. The most important 60 milliseconds in the game. An enemy that
#    takes damage lifts toward white for `hit_flash` seconds. The GLYPHS DO NOT
#    CHANGE and the sprite DOES NOT MOVE -- a flash that changes shape reads as a
#    different enemy arriving, not as the one you have as being hurt.
#    This is the ladder's only licensed violation: an enemy may pass the mote's
#    0.74 luminance for 60ms. Sixty milliseconds cannot be hunted through; a
#    permanent brightness can.
param
column4: seconds an enemy stays lifted after taking damage

column1: param
column4: fraction lifted toward white. 1.0 would erase the silhouette

column1: # 2. DEATH POP. One frame of the enemy's own glyphs in bright white, then the
#    gore decal takes the cell. Costs no art and it is the single frame that
#    sells a kill. At 40 kills/sec these overlap constantly and that is the point:
#    the field crackles.
param
column4: seconds

column1: # 3. DAMAGE NUMBERS. See design.md §14 for why there is AT MOST ONE PER ENEMY.
#    Short version: I already made this mistake with gore. One decal per kill
#    stacked two hundred decals on a busy cell and the floor turned into a red
#    sheet; the owner reported it. One number per damage EVENT is the same bug
#    with digits. At 14:00 that is ~200 enemies x 4 weapons x their cooldowns.
#    So: a number is born on an enemy's first damage and rises. Damage taken
#    while it is alive is ADDED to it, its life resets, and it gets brighter.
#    Two hundred rats give you two hundred numbers. A rat hit eleven times gives
#    you ONE number that climbs to 34 and glows.
#    Brightness-by-accumulation is also why this game does not need a crit system
#    to have a crit FEEL. It doesn't have one. Don't build one.
param
column4: seconds a number lives if it takes no further damage

column1: param
column4: world units it rises over its life (~1.5 rows; a cell is 2 wu tall)

column1: param
column4: brightness added each time damage is added to a live number

column1: param
column4: cap. a number may never out-shine the player

column1: param
column4: most numbers alive at once. over budget, drop the DIMMEST, not the oldest

column1: param
column4: damage below this prints nothing. a `0` floating up is a bug report

column1: # 4. HIT STOP. The simulation freezes; rendering does not. Fires ONLY when the
#    PLAYER takes damage. Not on enemy hits -- at 40 kills/sec the game would
#    judder permanently and you would never find out why it felt bad.
param
column4: seconds of frozen simulation when the player is hit

column1: param
column4: and when you level up. the pause IS the reward

column1: # 5. LEVEL-UP. No expanding ring. A ring would need a glyph, and every glyph I
#    could give it is already owned by the bolt, the mote or the gore. The `@`
#    flashes gold and the world stops for 80ms. Restraint is a choice here, not
#    a shortage: the card is about to fill the screen anyway.
param
column4: seconds the player's @ burns gold (`Y`) before the cards come up

column1: # 6. EMBERS -- the ambient one, and the direct answer to "unleash her creativity".
#    The lantern throws sparks. They rise, cool, and die. They damage nothing,
#    collide with nothing, and are drawn UNDER everything: if a spark and a rat
#    want the same cell, the rat gets it.
#    Spawned in the annulus between `ember_r_in` and the CURRENT light radius, so
#    Lantern Oil visibly widens the shower of sparks. That is a passive you can
#    see, which is worth more than a passive you can read.
param
column4: sparks per second

column1: param
column4: world units; no sparks inside this, the player's own cells stay clean

column1: param
column4: seconds

column1: param
column4: world units per second, upward, with a slight horizontal wander

column1: param
column4: hard cap

column1: param
column4: brightness ceiling. ALWAYS dimmer than a mote. beauty does not outrank information

column1: # 7. MOTE ABSORPTION. Inside the pickup radius a mote streaks to you. Give it a
#    short motion trail rather than more brightness -- §9's doctrine, and the
#    reason `mote_lift` came down from 0.35 to 0.10. Motion is free contrast.
param
column4: cells of trail behind an absorbing mote

column1: param
column4: trail brightness, relative to the mote

column1: # ---- shake rows: shake <event> <amp_cells> <seconds> -------------------------
# A character grid can only shake by a WHOLE CELL, which is 1 wu, which is an
# earthquake. This is the first thing we get back for leaving the terminal: the
# canvas can offset the field by a FRACTION of a cell, in pixels. So `amp` is in
# cells and it is meant to be less than one.
# The field viewport shakes. THE HUD DOES NOT. A health bar that moves is a
# health bar you cannot read at the exact moment you need to read it.
# Decay to zero over `seconds`, linear is fine. And note what is NOT in this list:
# ordinary hits. A screen that shakes constantly is a screen you stop reading.
# Four events in a twenty-minute run. Each one should make you sit up.
shake
column5: she winds up. you have time to move

column1: shake
shake
column5: you were dead and you are not

column1: shake
# Read glyphs.tsv's header first: three laws, and law 1b says everything the
# player EMITS is part of the Warden's alphabet. These are the juice layer's
# characters and nothing else in the game may use them.
# `.` is retired from the entire game. At a glance a baseline dot IS `·`, and
# `·` is an XP mote, and the owner has already told us once that he cannot find
# his XP. The bolt used to shrink to `.` as it died; it now fades in colour and
# keeps its shape. Fading is what the canvas is FOR.
glyph
column4: w
column5: the numbers layer's alphabet. no field sprite may contain a digit

column1: glyph
column4: y
column5: rising spark. cools `y`->`r`->`e` over its life

column1: glyph
column4: W
column5: the Warden's seeking bolt, at every age. never `.`

column1: glyph
column4: Y
column5: a Cinder Trail ember. a WEAPON: it burns things. never `.`
